text: It is unfortunate that minority populations are vastly underrepresented in studies relating to cancer and chronic diseases. This issue has turned into a global crisis due to the rise in cancer and chronic disease rates. In the United States the reasons for the lack of participants in trials and studies vary but are often due to many different barriers which often include lack of trust in the medical institution. In order to engage the participation of minority populations a strategy that is culturally and socially relevant is necessary and it is essential to understand the historical, cultural, social, and economic issues in each particular community. Through years of research in many countries, we developed the model of using community residents as the core of the research staff and as the initial point of contact for participants. Using this approach, the lab moved to the Texas Rio Grande Valley in 2001 in order to study chronic diseases. In 2004, a group of local women were trained to carry out a longitudinal study on 5,000 Mexican Americans recruited from random selected households. The success of this initial group of participants and staff led to further training and skills that were needed to carry out complex studies such as the carrying out of ultrasonography, FibroScan, retinal scans for eye diseases and cognitive assessments for dementia studies. Presently, this local workforce is involved in several ongoing trials funded by the NIH for a study on the prevention of chronic diseases through clinical trials and multi-omic studies of liver diseases in individuals with obesity and diabetes. Through the use of local personnel, precision medicine approaches are being further explored to the studies of prevention and treatment of chronic diseases in minority populations.
headline: Fully Human Written

Through years of research in many countries, we developed the model of using community residents as the core of the research staff and as the initial point of contact for participants. Using this approach, the lab moved to the Texas Rio Grande Valley in 2001 in order to study chronic diseases. The success of this initial group of participants and staff led to further training and skills that were needed to carry out complex studies such as the carrying out of ultrasonography, FibroScan, retinal scans for eye diseases and cognitive assessments for dementia studies. Presently, this local workforce is involved in several ongoing trials funded by the NIH for a study on the prevention of chronic diseases through clinical trials and multi-omic studies of liver diseases in individuals with obesity and diabetes. Through the use of local personnel, precision medicine approaches are being further explored to the studies of prevention and treatment of chronic diseases in minority populations.
